title: Hearty Sausage Stew
description: Italian sausage broken into chunks and slow-cooked with white beans, Swiss chard, cinnamon, and Madeira. Rich, deeply flavored, and built for dunking bread into.

ingredients.name: Italian sausages, skins removed, broken into small pieces
Madeira, sweet sherry, beer, or white wine
whole canned tomatoes (or 1 can fire-roasted crushed tomatoes)
white beans, canned and drained (cannellini or butter beans)
Swiss chard or Tuscan kale (cavolo nero), stalks separated from leaves

instructions.text: Slice the skins of the sausages and remove the meat. Break roughly into small meatball-sized pieces. Finely slice garlic, celery, and onions.
Get a large heavy-based pan hot. Drizzle in olive oil and once warm, add the sausage. Fry a few minutes to release the fat and get some color on the meat.

Turn heat right down. Add garlic, red pepper flakes, rosemary, bay leaves, and cinnamon. Keep the heat low and gentle -- this stage is about slowly infusing flavor into the oil. Don't let the garlic color.

Pour in the Madeira to deglaze (add early if garlic is starting to color). Scrape up the fond with a wooden spoon. Add onions, celery, and crush in the tomatoes. Season generously, mix well, and cook on a gentle heat for 10–12 minutes until onions are sweet and softened.
Add beans and pour in the stock. Bring to a gentle simmer and cook 20–30 minutes until the broth thickens and the flavors come together.
Chop the Swiss chard stalks into 2cm pieces. Add to the broth and simmer a few minutes. Then add the leaves and stir through. Put the lid on, turn off the heat, and leave for 5 minutes.

Remove lid, taste, and adjust -- more red pepper flakes if the warmth isn't there, more salt if the broth needs richness. Ladle generously into bowls. Serve with thick slices of buttered bread for dunking.

notes:
The cinnamon stick is not optional -- it adds a subtle warmth that ties everything together without tasting like a dessert. Remove before serving.

Madeira is the classic choice but sweet sherry, white wine, or even beer all work. Use what you have.

Separating chard stalks from leaves matters -- stalks go in first as they need more time; leaves wilt almost instantly.
